title: Insertion in Linked List

content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: a line with integer N, the number of nodes in the initial linked list; a line with N space‑separated integers, the values of the nodes (in the order they are given); a line with integer X, the value to be inserted at the beginning of the list.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the values of the linked list after the insertion, starting from the head. Values are separated by a single space. A trailing space is allowed.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The initial values are inserted at the front one by one, producing the reversed list 3 2 1. After inserting 4 at the front the list becomes 4 3 2 1.
Values 5 and 10 are inserted at the front, yielding 10 5. Inserting 7 at the front results in 7 10 5.
Single initial node 20 becomes the list. After inserting 15 at the front the list is 15 20.

solution.approach: For each test case we simulate the behaviour of inserting nodes at the front of a singly linked list. Inserting at the front reverses the order of the initially given values because each new node becomes the new head. We therefore read the N values and prepend each to a deque (or list) to obtain the reversed order. After processing the initial list we prepend the extra value X. Finally we output the deque from head to tail. This mimics exactly what the provided reference implementation does. The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and uses O(N) auxiliary space for storing the list.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(total N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) auxiliary space per test case for the list representation.

tcGeneratorPython:
def generate_case(i, RINT, POWER):
    # i is the test case index (0 <= i <= 20).
    # Use RINT(a,b) for random integer in [a,b] and POWER(a,b) for a**b.
    if i == 0:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        n = 1
        print(n)
        print(1)
        print(1)
    elif i == 1:
        t = 1
        print(t)
        n = 1
        print(n)
        print(1000)
        print(1000)
    # Add further generation logic for other indices as needed.
    # For hidden cases the generator is not used by the evaluator; inputs are pre‑written.
